content:
# Matthew Chapter 27 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_027_page_001.jpg, MAT_027_page_002.jpg, MAT_027_page_003.jpg

### Judas Hangs Himself

1. Very early in the morning, the chief priests and the elders met together to plan how to have Jesus executed.

2. They bound him, led him away, and handed him over to Pilate, the Roman governor.

3. When Judas, who had betrayed him, saw that Jesus had been condemned to death, he was filled with remorse. He took the thirty pieces of silver back to the chief priests and elders.

4. "I have sinned," he said. "I have betrayed an innocent man." "What do we care?" they replied. "That's your problem."

5. So Judas threw the silver coins into the temple, left, and went out and hanged himself.

6. The chief priests picked up the coins. "It's against the law to put this into the temple treasury," they said, "since it's blood money."

7. After some discussion, they used the money to buy the potter's field as a cemetery for foreigners.

8. That is why it has been called the Field of Blood to this day.

9. This fulfilled the prophecy of Jeremiah: "They took the thirty pieces of silver — the price at which he was valued by the people of Israel —

10. and used the money to buy the potter's field, as the Lord directed me."

### Jesus Before Pilate

11. Now Jesus stood before the governor, and the governor asked him, "Are you the King of the Jews?" Jesus replied, "You have said it."

12. But when the chief priests and elders brought their accusations against him, Jesus said nothing.

13. "Don't you hear all these charges they are bringing against you?" Pilate demanded.

14. But Jesus made no response to any of the charges, much to the governor's astonishment.

15. Now it was the governor's custom during the festival to release one prisoner chosen by the crowd.

16. At that time they had a notorious prisoner named Barabbas.

17. So when the crowd gathered, Pilate asked them, "Which one do you want me to release to you — Barabbas, or Jesus who is called the Messiah?"

18. He knew very well that they had handed Jesus over because of envy.

19. While Pilate was sitting on the judgment seat, his wife sent him a message: "Leave that innocent man alone. I suffered terribly in a dream last night because of him."

20. Meanwhile, the chief priests and elders had persuaded the crowds to ask for Barabbas and to demand that Jesus be put to death.

21. "Which of the two do you want me to release to you?" the governor asked. "Barabbas!" they shouted.

22. "Then what should I do with Jesus who is called the Messiah?" Pilate asked. They all shouted, "Crucify him!"

23. "Why? What crime has he committed?" Pilate asked. But they just screamed louder, "Crucify him!"

24. When Pilate saw that he was getting nowhere and that a riot was developing, he took water and washed his hands in front of the crowd. "I am innocent of this man's blood," he said. "The responsibility is yours."

25. And all the people answered, "Let his blood be on us and on our children!"

26. So Pilate released Barabbas to them. He had Jesus flogged, then handed him over to be crucified.

### The Soldiers Mock Jesus

27. The governor's soldiers took Jesus into their headquarters and called out the entire battalion.

28. They stripped him and put a scarlet robe on him.

29. They wove a crown of thorns and shoved it on his head, and they put a stick in his right hand as a mock scepter. Then they knelt before him in mockery and taunted, "Hail, King of the Jews!"

30. They spat on him and grabbed the stick and beat him on the head with it.

31. When they were finally tired of mocking him, they took off the robe and put his own clothes back on him. Then they led him away to be crucified.

### The Crucifixion

32. Along the way, they grabbed a man from Cyrene named Simon and forced him to carry Jesus' cross.

33. They went to a place called Golgotha, which means "Place of the Skull."

34. There they offered him wine mixed with bitter gall, but when he had tasted it, he refused to drink it.

35. After they had nailed him to the cross, they divided his clothes among themselves by throwing dice.

36. Then they sat down and kept guard over him.

37. A sign was fastened above his head, announcing the charge against him: THIS IS JESUS, THE KING OF THE JEWS.

38. Two criminals were crucified with him, one on his right and one on his left.

39. People passing by hurled insults at him, shaking their heads

40. and saying, "So! You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days — save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross!"

41. The chief priests, along with the teachers of the law and the elders, also mocked him.

42. "He saved others," they scoffed, "but he can't save himself! So he's the King of Israel, is he? Let him come down from the cross right now, and we'll believe in him!

43. He trusts in God — let God rescue him now if he wants him! After all, he said, 'I am the Son of God.'"

44. Even the criminals who were crucified with him heaped the same insults on him.

### The Death of Jesus

45. From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness fell across the whole land.

46. At about three o'clock, Jesus cried out in a loud voice, "Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani?" — which means, "My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?"

47. Some of the bystanders misunderstood and thought he was calling for Elijah.

48. One of them ran and filled a sponge with sour wine, put it on a stick, and held it up for him to drink.

49. But the rest said, "Leave him alone. Let's see if Elijah comes to save him."

50. Then Jesus shouted out again and gave up his spirit.

51. At that moment, the curtain of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom. The earth shook, rocks split apart,

52. and tombs broke open. The bodies of many godly people who had died were raised to life.

53. They left the cemetery after Jesus' resurrection, went into Jerusalem, and appeared to many people.

54. The Roman officer and the other soldiers keeping guard over Jesus were terrified by the earthquake and all that had happened. "This man truly was the Son of God!" they said.

55. Many women who had followed Jesus from Galilee to care for him were watching from a distance.

56. Among them were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joses, and the mother of the sons of Zebedee.

### The Burial of Jesus

57. As evening approached, a rich man from Arimathea arrived. His name was Joseph, and he had become a disciple of Jesus.

58. He went to Pilate and asked for Jesus' body. Pilate issued an order to release it to him.

59. Joseph took the body, wrapped it in a clean linen cloth,

60. and placed it in his own new tomb, which had been carved out of rock. He rolled a great stone across the entrance and left.

61. Both Mary Magdalene and the other Mary were sitting there across from the tomb, watching.

### The Guard at the Tomb

62. The next day — the day after the Preparation — the chief priests and Pharisees went to see Pilate.

63. "Sir," they said, "we remember that while that deceiver was still alive, he said, 'After three days I will rise from the dead.'

64. So please order that the tomb be sealed until the third day. Otherwise, his disciples may come and steal his body and tell everyone, 'He has risen from the dead!' Then we would be worse off than before."

65. Pilate replied, "Take a guard. Go and make the tomb as secure as you know how."

66. So they went and sealed the tomb and posted guards to protect it.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 4 — "That's your problem":** The religious leaders' response to Judas is breathtaking in its callousness. They were perfectly willing to use him as a tool for betrayal but accept zero moral responsibility for the outcome. Judas sought absolution from the wrong people.

**Verse 25 — "His blood be on us and on our children":** This verse has been catastrophically misused throughout history to justify antisemitism. In context, it is the cry of a specific crowd at a specific moment, manipulated by their leaders (v.20). Matthew is not condemning a people but describing a tragic scene. Any reading that turns this verse into a blanket condemnation contradicts everything Jesus taught about forgiveness.

**Verse 46 — "My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?":** Jesus quotes Psalm 22:1 — not randomly, but invoking a psalm that begins with anguish and ends with vindication (Ps. 22:22-31). Yet the cry is real, not theatrical. In this moment, Jesus experiences what it feels like for God to be absent. This is the lowest point of the incarnation — God-forsakenness on behalf of the God-forsaken.

**Verses 51-53 — Cosmic signs:** The torn curtain, earthquake, split rocks, and raised saints are apocalyptic signs. The temple curtain (separating the Holy of Holies from the rest of the temple) tears from top to bottom — God is the one tearing it. Access to God's presence is now open. The earthquake and resurrection of saints preview the general resurrection. In Matthew's telling, Jesus' death does not merely change theology — it changes the fabric of reality.

**Verse 54 — The centurion's confession:** A Roman soldier — the agent of Jesus' execution — becomes the first person in the Passion narrative to say what is true: "This man truly was the Son of God." The outsider sees what the insiders refused to see.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
